questions.question: A covert biological release differs from most other disasters because:
options: A. It is always announced in advance
B. Its 'scene' is a pattern of illness in the population, revealed after an incubation period
C. It causes immediate visible casualties at one location
D. It requires no public-health involvement
questions.rationale: Incubation periods mean a covert event surfaces as scattered illness over days; the detectors are clinicians and epidemiologists recognizing the pattern, not responders at a scene.

questions.question: Which is a Category A bioterrorism agent?
options: A. Salmonella
B. Anthrax
C. Ricin
D. Common cold virus
questions.rationale: Anthrax is Category A ('A B PLoT': Anthrax, Botulism, Plague, smallpox, Tularemia, viral hemorrhagic fevers) — high mortality with special preparedness needs.

questions.question: Which set lists Category A agents?
options: A. Anthrax, botulism, plague, smallpox, tularemia, viral hemorrhagic fevers
B. Influenza, measles, mumps, rubella
C. Salmonella, E. coli, cholera, ricin
D. HIV, hepatitis, tuberculosis, malaria
questions.rationale: The Category A group is anthrax, botulism, plague, smallpox, tularemia, and viral hemorrhagic fevers — the highest-priority threats.

questions.question: Which epidemiologic finding should raise suspicion of an intentional biological event?
options: A. A few influenza cases during flu season
B. A large point-source cluster of a severe illness that is unusual for the area and season
C. One case of the common cold
D. Seasonal allergy complaints
questions.rationale: Point-source clusters, unusual diseases for the region/season, and unexpected severity are classic red flags for an intentional or unusual biological event.

questions.question: A patient with suspected smallpox requires which precautions?
options: A. Contact only
B. Droplet only
C. Airborne precautions (N95+ respirator and negative-pressure room) plus contact
D. Standard precautions only
questions.rationale: Smallpox spreads by the airborne route (and contact with lesions), requiring a fitted respirator, negative-pressure isolation, and contact precautions.

questions.question: What is the difference between isolation and quarantine?
options: A. They are the same
B. Isolation separates the ill; quarantine restricts the exposed-but-well
C. Isolation is for the exposed; quarantine is for the ill
D. Both apply only to responders
questions.rationale: Isolation applies to people who are sick; quarantine applies to people who were exposed but are not yet symptomatic.

questions.question: A clinician strongly suspects a case of inhalational anthrax. The most important early action is to:
options: A. Wait for blood cultures before telling anyone
B. Notify public health immediately while isolating and protecting staff
C. Discharge the patient to avoid alarm
D. Treat and say nothing until three cases appear
questions.rationale: Early notification — before confirmation — lets public health investigate, initiate prophylaxis for others, and mount the response. Delay costs exposed people their prophylaxis window.

questions.question: Antibiotics given to people exposed to anthrax before they become ill is an example of:
options: A. Isolation
B. Post-exposure prophylaxis
C. Quarantine
D. Crisis standards of care
questions.rationale: Preventive treatment of the exposed-but-well to stop disease from developing is post-exposure prophylaxis, often dispensed at PODs.

questions.question: The Strategic National Stockpile exists to:
options: A. Store weapons
B. Supplement local medications, vaccines, and supplies during large public-health emergencies
C. Replace hospital pharmacies permanently
D. Fund research
questions.rationale: The SNS is a national cache deployed to bolster local resources during major events — for example, antibiotics or vaccines for mass prophylaxis.

questions.question: Which PPE is appropriate for a patient with pneumonic plague (droplet spread)?
options: A. Airborne precautions with negative pressure
B. Droplet precautions — surgical mask within close range plus standard precautions
C. No precautions
D. Contact precautions only
questions.rationale: Pneumonic plague transmits by respiratory droplets at close range, so droplet precautions (surgical mask) are used — reserving airborne PPE for truly airborne agents.

questions.question: During a pandemic with constrained supplies, protecting the workforce includes:
options: A. Removing all PPE requirements to save stock
B. PPE with conservation/extended-use strategies, vaccination, and mental-health support
C. Sending symptomatic staff to work
D. Ignoring staffing sustainability
questions.rationale: A months-long event depends on the workforce: appropriate PPE (with conservation when scarce), vaccination, and mental-health/sustainable-staffing measures keep responders functional.

questions.question: Non-pharmaceutical interventions (NPIs) are MOST important:
options: A. Never — only drugs work
B. Before specific vaccines or therapeutics are available
C. Only after the pandemic ends
D. Only for responders
questions.rationale: Distancing, hygiene, masking, and isolation/quarantine are the primary tools to slow spread early, especially before targeted vaccines or treatments exist.

questions.question: Simultaneous outbreaks of the same unusual illness in three distant cities most strongly suggests:
options: A. Ordinary seasonal illness
B. A possible intentional/multi-site biological release warranting public-health investigation
C. Laboratory error only
D. No cause for concern
questions.rationale: Geographically separate simultaneous clusters of the same rare disease are a hallmark epidemiologic clue for an intentional or point-source event.

questions.question: When the transmission route of a suspected biological agent is unknown, the safest PPE approach is to:
options: A. Use standard precautions only
B. Default to a higher level of protection until the route is known
C. Use no PPE to avoid alarming patients
D. Wait for lab results before wearing PPE
questions.rationale: Uncertainty warrants higher precaution: it is safer to step down PPE once the agent/route is characterized than to under-protect against an airborne threat.

questions.question: Crisis standards of care are invoked when:
options: A. A single patient is critically ill
B. Demand overwhelms resources, requiring formally declared allocation of scarce resources for population benefit
C. A hospital wants to reduce documentation
D. Staff prefer a simpler workflow
questions.rationale: Crisis standards are a declared, framework-governed shift used only when needs exceed resources, allocating scarce resources to maximize population benefit.

questions.question: Which best captures the clinician's pivotal role in a covert biological event?
options: A. Waiting for authorities to announce the event
B. Recognizing an unusual case/pattern and reporting it early to trigger the public-health response
C. Managing the scene perimeter
D. Performing decontamination
questions.rationale: Because covert events surface in clinics and EDs, the alert clinician who recognizes and reports an unusual illness is frequently what starts the entire response.
